sentences.text: Well. mostly Yiddish and Polish.
My father spoke a very fluent Polish, because he always dealt with the government, with majors and, and colonels.
Mostly military people were in the business that my father had, so, he spoke a very fluent Polish.
So did my mother.
Probably my brown eyes, because the nose they could not see.
I put my cowl of my coat up, and with a muffler, I tied mine-- because it was so cold.
So I pretended I'm breathing in through the muffler, and half of my nose was not seen.
So, only, probably, the dark eyes could have, you know--
Well, I think that most of them did not have dark hair, and dark eyes, as the Jews had.
And I think that the Germans could not determine the Jews as much as the Poles did.
They used to show mostly the Jews to the-- they used to emphasize and point the Jews to the Germans.
Because, to the Germans, a Pole or a Jew, they really could not tell the difference.
Oh, definitely.
Definitely.
They could.
Sure.
They could-- they could not-- that was before the war, too.
They did not attack Poles on the street.
They attacked the Jews, not because they were dressed differently.
I'm not talking about the Jews that wore special dress, special men.
But young children were going to a park and they were thrown stones at because they were Jewish, and Polacks-- the Polish children recognized them.
No.
The building we lived, there were quite, quite a few Gentile families living in that building, and somehow the, the parents used to get along fine.
But, apparently, some of the children were just taught-- either in school, or in church, or who knows where-- to hate the Jew.
Well, I think that's the German Jews were more assimilated in Germany.
They tried to-- I don't know how much intermarriage was at the time when I, I knew Germany.
Or, the way of living.
They tried to fit in.
Where the Polish Jews, to my regret-- although, my father also wore a special garb as a religious Jew.
And I think that's why they were more recognized on the street than any other Jews.
And the girls, the only-- the only way I can tell is because of the Semitic looks-- the nose, the eyes, the hair.
I don't know what it was that made them recognize the Jews.
I don't think that the young people were self-conscious.
The older generation, maybe.
But I think the young, young people went to school and were educated and we mingled with-- although I went to a religious school, but I never strayed away from non-Jews.
And we were never taught at home to stay away from non-Jewish kids.
We played with them.
And, as a matter of fact, I told you, I helped that girl out-- the one that was-- lived next, next house from me.
I used to do all her homework.
When the war broke out, she passed by me on the street, she did not even see me.
I do remember, because at this age, you know, when you-- when you're 15, 16 years old, you remember these things, and they were hurting.
Yes, because the neighbors knew you most.
They knew who you were.
They came-- they came on a holiday.
They came to taste your food, because they liked it.
But in the long run, they hated the Jew.
Yes.
Yes.
First of all, we had the woman that was our housekeeper for many years.
And we-- she got married.
And was a system in Poland that you had a seamstress come into the house and do the trousseau.
Well, the same way as my mother did for my sister, she did for her, too.
And they opened a little store-- she and her husband opened a little store, because she saved up the money that she was paid for.
And when the war broke out, she was the one to bring things to us.
She remembered the children-- well, she, she almost raised us.
And if she could find something, till they closed the ghetto, she used to bring things in for us.
But the time they closed the ghetto, there was no way of getting anything in.
And--
No, I went back with my mother.
My father tried to come but he was, a few times, sent back-- beaten up and sent back.
But somehow my-- there was a border, because Lodz belonged to Germany where Warsaw belonged to Poland, occupied by Germans.
So, my mother somehow got through.
And going back, my mother-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] my-- Just tell-- so you went back with your mother to Lodz.
Yeah, to the ghetto.
They, they were-- my family was already-- my father, mother, and my sisters-- in the ghetto already.
And that-- a week later my sister Regina, who lives here, was diagnosed with typhoid fever, and she was taken to a hospital.
At that time, we knew that somebody's taken to the hospital, she will never come back.
So, I was running after, and I blamed myself that because I came to ghetto, she got sick.
But got, got-- we got help.
She came back from the hospital.
She recovered.
And we were together, and we worked in the ghetto.
And I, I got a position-- not a position, I got a job-- in a factory as a-- I had to bring an electric iron, which I found from somebody.
And I became a, a presser.
And I had to press 20 pairs of military pants a day.
Was Rumkowski already in charge of the ghetto at this point?
Yes, Rumkowski was in charge.
When I came into the ghetto, he was already in charge of--
Well, subconsciously-- I don't know.
I thought that I was not there when they was moved from-- and they went through a great ordeal.
And I blamed myself that I was not with them when they were moved from the city to the ghetto.
And here I come into the ghetto, and my sister takes so gravely ill.
So I don't know, it's-- maybe I was childish.
I don't know what it was.
But I just blamed myself.
I says, my God, because I came into the ghetto, maybe because of that, she got sick.
But when she came home, it was a great relief, and I never thought of it again that it was my fault.
But you always-- there was a time-- I was very unhappy in the ghetto to begin with.
So maybe this was something to blame yourself for coming back from freedom to a ghetto, that this made me think like that.
Because other people did become ill also.
Yes.
Sure, there was a hospital full of people.
And, thank God, she recovered.
And then we could keep-- we had one room that we cooked in and slept in and--
We were two-- four sisters, and my brother was, in the beginning, with us, and then my father and mother.
And everything was done in this room.
My mother cooked and, and washed the laundry, and whatever was necessary.
But we made the best out of it.
Well, to tell you, in the winter-- inside of the room, and the corner, you could see icicles inside.
So, it was not the greatest-- the greatest condition.
We made the best out of it.
We had no wood, we had no coal-- we had a coal stove.
But we had no coal or no wood.
So, somehow, wherever we could, we got pieces of wood to, to cook something.
Was very little to cook.
I remember at one time-- this was already later on-- that my mother and I worked in the factory very, very hard.
I stood eight hours on my feet, pressing the inside, press the seams, and everything had to be just like for, for the officers' pants.
And had to be-- 20 pairs of pants have to be finished at that time.
And my mother cooked a soup from a half a pound parsley and water.
No flour, nothing was in the house.
And when she gave us the soup to eat, she says, I wish I could give you a piece of bread to eat with that soup.
But I'm sorry, we won't have anything for tomorrow morning.
And I remember it like so distinctly that I moved my chair back from the table.
And I says, mom, how can you talk about bread, I am so full.
I couldn't eat a bite, my stomach was full of water.
Yes.
Definitely, because we, we could see the tears in her eyes.
That she felt very badly about us.
We were young and growing, and she could not feed us.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
So then we-- I worked there for-- from 1940-- '41, I think I started.
And I worked, sometimes, day and night, and another day.
Eight hours each.
In the morning, after the night shift, I took a cup of coffee to drink.
It was ersatz coffee.
I don't know you know what ersatz coffee, it's-- The Israeli--
No, no, it's make-believe, what the Germans cooked, and dried it, and then they send it to the ghetto.
It had no taste.
I took a cup of coffee and washed my face, so my eyes should not fall-- I should not fall asleep while I was working.
Now, for that I got an extra portion.
We used to get a quarter of a pound of bread, and five ounces of sausage.
And for that we payed like thirty-- whatever money that was.
We did not eat non-kosher.
So, the sausage I sold for the amount that I had to pay for the bread and the sausage, and the bread I took home to share with the family.
So, in the three times, I accumulate like 60 ounces of-- it was not ounces, it was [INAUDIBLE] of bread, and I didn't have to pay anything, because what I sold the sausage I could pay for it.
And the hunger was great.
I mean, it was getting worse, and worse, and it was very cold.
We had nothing to heat the, the room with.
And then, it came a time that they started sending people out.
Well, what Rumkowski did-- whatever the Germans send in work, he was supposed to get food to feed the people.
He did not distribute the food according to what the Germans wanted him to distribute.
He kept it for a dark, rainy day.
And he-- once they sent rationed potatoes that came in to the ghetto.
And they was laying outside, and they were not distributed to people.
And the rain came down, and they just fell apart.
They rotted.
They-- people started running to grab something, and they knew that people are going to get typhoid fever from it.
So, they poured Clorox on it, and they took-- and took it to, to graves, and threw it in the grave, and covered it up with [INAUDIBLE].
A lot of people could have eaten that time, those potatoes, if they would be given, rationed out.
Yes, I do blame Rumkowski very much.
He was a very stubborn man, and I knew-- I knew Rumkowski before the war-- from before the war.
He was an older man that took care on the orphanage.
And he was going around collecting money.
That's where I know him from.
He used to come to my father to collect money for that orphanage in Helenovek.
And I knew him from coming year after year to our house, and my father used to support that orphanage.
He did an awful lot that time for the orphans.
As a matter of fact, in the ghetto, the one that was the Vermittler, they called him, the Bruder of the Juden-- the brother of the Jews-- the Germans used to call him.
He used to have everything that was going on between the Germans and the Jews, he took care-- He was raised in an orphanage, and Rumkowski made him like the head of the working class, you know that--
So, there was food coming in.
Rumkowski said, once, he saved a few children, and [INAUDIBLE] my sister was working at that time, in a-- in a drugstore.
And he said, if he only save-- will save 10 to 20 children from the whole ghetto, he will be happy to show the world that he saved 20 children.
He could have saved a lot of people.
But he almost did, didn't he?
No.
If the war would have ended earlier, if the Germans, if he would not give so many people out.
If the German-- when the Germans ask for, for that amount of people, he never said no.
I don't know if you could say to the Germans, no, I don't want to give you that amount of people.
He tried to have a lot of-- enough work for the people to be able to keep them.
But if somebody lost the parents, and this, it hurted-- although I was with my parents to the last minute-- we went to the-- out of the ghetto.
But there were children that lost their parents.
They were the first ones to send out from the ghetto.
And he knew-- he knew where these people are going.
Because there were single people without parents, they should not have been punished for that, and be sent to Majdanek or Treblinka, or Auschwitz, or whatever they send them out.
Yes.
I think it might-- I think because he thought, in a way, sometimes, maybe, deep down in his heart he did not want to part a family.
He did not want to send one person away from the family.
If it was a single person, he substituted.
Maybe.

I don't know if, if he deserves to be, you know, rationalized like that-- that he did something good, because he really could do much better, or he could have resigned and give away to a younger person.
Because-- Not that I condemned.
He probably was not much older than I am now.
And I'm, I'm still-- I hope that I'm still by clear mind that he was at that time.
But, but I think that the Germans took advantage of his age.
He actually appointed himself.
Yes.
They had a meeting, from what I understand, I was not that time in, in, in, in Lodz anymore.
But there was, was a Jewish Gemeinde-- Jewish-- not organization, what is it, like, a Jewish--
Yeah, like a Jewish council.
And they ask, who is the oldest?
Now, I don't know if he didn't speak German, but to him, he was the oldest, by age.
They meant who is the leader of the Jewish community.
And he said, that, that he is the oldest, so he was appointed to-- the eldest of the ghetto.
But in-- to so some people, he was very good.
And he saw my father once, at the round-up-- that my father worked in the kitchen, and they took-- that time he came to take out the young people and send them to factories.
So he noticed my father, and he asked him to come to see him.
My father did not want to go, because my father did not believe that a person can help.
Only God will help.
But we made him go because we knew that if he wouldn't-- won't listen, what he asked him to do, we will be sent out, the whole family, from the ghetto.
He had that much power.
So, he offered my father a job as a dir-- head of a-- of the kitchen.

And my father would not accept it, because he said they're cooking on Saturday, and he would not be there on Saturdays because the factories were-- you know, people were working, they had to be given the soup on that Saturday.
And my father remained the way he was at night.
He was working at night.
You know, a [GERMAN].
And this gave him the time to sit.
He was a very learned man, so he had the chance to learn the Torah at night, you know, when he was there.
And this was satisfactory to him, that-- Then we-- they started liquidating the ghetto and we escaped.
There was-- there was a-- out-- almost out of the ghetto, but was still in the ghetto, they called it a-- what do they call it?
It will come back to me.
And we went there, and we worked-- we worked on the cemetery.
We brought my father there, my father was very sick.
He had diarrhea, and we brought him on a wheelbarrow in the middle of the night.
It was pitch black, and we brought him there.
And he was with us.
And every morning, we would go into the cemetery.
They were-- the Germans knew that they were liquidating the ghetto, and there were a lot of brushes and trees, and they were afraid that people will hide there.
So they took some men to cut out the trees, and the, the girls and the women were pulling it away and tying it up.
And they gave us that time so much to eat that everybody got sick just from that much food, we could not digest any longer.
And this lasted-- and then one day they asked us to, to leave the cemetery.
And a black wagon drove into the cemetery, and we heard two shots.
And maybe five, ten minutes later, that black wagon went out-- left the cemetery-- and they told us we can go back.
Later we found out that there were two brothers that used to give out to the Germans Jew-- names of Jewish people who had money, or had something.
And they didn't want to leave them, so they wouldn't tell the world what the Germans were doing.
So they killed them.
They shot them on the cemetery and they were buried there.
And that's what the Germans did with all the people that, you know, used to bring all the information to them.
They used to kill them after that.
And the Germans, once they came in-- they used to come in in the morning-- to get people, to send them out.
And by 5 o'clock, they used to leave.
So everybody used to come out like, from, from the basements.
You know, the worms come out from the, the-- and people started cooking and preparing some-- whatever they cook, food or something.
One day, at 5 o'clock, my little sister-- should rest in peace-- went outside, and she heard-- overheard two policemen saying that they were called to the station.
And she came in and said, let's leave the room right now, because the Germans are coming back.
She was only 7 years old.
And I said to her, get out of the house because you are black, black crow-- you're bringing back bad news.
They never come back after 5 o'clock.
Didn't take five minutes, people were running in all directions and the German came back, and took that time maybe 500 or 600 people, and sent them to Treblinka.
Definitely intuition.
This child could feel more than any adult.
And she saved us because we escaped from the room and we went to [INAUDIBLE] that's how we came to [INAUDIBLE] that time.
And we were saved there another, another few weeks because we worked on the cemetery there.
And came-- on a Saturday they announced that complete-- that the police is already send out, and we have to go to the train.
And we would have-- we could have saved ourselves, because we were saved on an attic-- in an attic in a candy factory.
And we went out from there and went to the train.

And there was a-- there was a family there, that father was a rabbi, and one of the daughters says-- and I was the youngest from-- besides my sisters-- and she says, well, wherever Sally goes we go, too.
She knows where to go.
And believe me I did not know where we are going.
When we are passing the coal mines in Poland-- [INAUDIBLE], that's Silesia, I think-- they said to me, see, if we would not listen to you, we would go to Auschwitz.
And this way they taking out to the coal mines.
But we did arrive in Auschwitz.
Yes, on that journey.
On that journey, we arrived to our-- we were crossing-- passing, passing the coal mines.
And then from the coal mines, we came-- we came to Auschwitz.
Auschwitz, yes.
And I was--
That's correct.
And when we-- when we boarded the trains, a German was-- when-- his boots with-- I don't know what you call, like a--
A whip.
He was just hitting his boots with the whip and says, [SPEAKING GERMAN]-- excuse me for the expression.
That means we, we-- you know, we, we told you-- because people have told us that we are going to go to a work camp-- and he knew that we are going to Auschwitz.
So he said that, you know, we fooled you, in a worse way to say that.
But the compartment that we were in was the last compartment, and there were only-- each one was, like, 50 or 60 people.
We were only, like, 30-some people.
But there were no windows.
And I knew that we are go-- I did not know, but I felt we are going to go on a long journey with no air.
And they came to close the, the, the doors.
And I put my foot in between.
How much does German care, cares-- a German care if he breaks my leg, or something.
But they didn't close the door.
And the older people that were on the train started screaming at me, they are going to kill us because you are rebelling.
And, get away from that door.
I says, no I don't want to die from, from-- you know, without air.
And, what they did, they brought a chain, and they chained to-- and they left that much of the door open.
And we had fresh air the whole night.
Our car-- our car was the only car that had the door open.
You could not jump from it because there was not enough room.
They just opened that much.
They put a chain with a lock.
And we could see everything.
We, we, we were the first one to see what's going on in Auschwitz.
When we got off the train, I saw that whoever was walking by there had no hair, or little-- or short hair.
